html:
V

Seaweed

I

тАЬComplexes,тАЭ read Mrs.┬аHilary, тАЬare of all sorts and sizes.тАЭ And there was a picture of four of them in a row, looking like netted cherry trees whose nets have got entangled with each other. So that was what they were like. Mrs.┬аHilary had previously thought of them as being more of the nature of noxious insects, or fibrous growths with infinite ramifications. Slim young trees. Not so bad, then, after all.

тАЬA complex is characterised, and its elements are bound together by a specific emotional tone, experienced as feeling when the complex is aroused. Apart from the mental processes and corresponding actions depending on purely rational mental systems, it is through complexes that the typical mental process (the specific response) works, the particular complex representing the particular set of mental elements involved in the process which begins with perception and cognition and ends with the corresponding conation.тАЭ

Mrs.┬аHilary read it three times, and the third time she understood it, if possible, less than the first. Complexes seemed very difficult things, and she had never been clever. Any of her children, or even her grandchildren, would understand it all in a moment. If you have such thingsтБатАФand everyone has, she had learntтБатАФyou ought to be able to understand them. Yet why? You didnтАЩt understand your bodily internal growths; you left them to your doctor. There were doctors who explained your complexes to you.тБатАКтБатАж What a revolting idea! It would surely make them worse, not better. (Mrs.┬аHilary still vaguely regarded these growths as something of the nature of cancer.)

Sometimes she imagined herself a patient, interviewing one of these odd doctors. A man doctor, not a woman; she didnтАЩt trust woman doctors of any kind; she had always been thankful that Neville had given it up and married instead.

тАЬInsomnia,тАЭ she would say, in these imaginary interviews, because that was so easy to start off with.

тАЬYou have something on your mind,тАЭ said the doctor. тАЬYou suffer from depression.тАЭ

тАЬYes, I know that. I was coming to that. That is what you must cure for me.тАЭ

тАЬYou must think back.тБатАКтБатАж What is the earliest thing you can remember? Perhaps your baptism? Possibly even your first bath? It has been done.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬYou may be right. I remember some early baths. One of them may have been the first of all, who knows? What of it, doctor?тАЭ

But the doctor, in her imaginings, would at this point only make notes in a big book and keep silence, as if he had thought as much. Perhaps, no more than she, he did not know what of it.

Mrs.┬аHilary could hear herself protesting.

тАЬI am not unhappy because of my baptism, which, so far as I know, went off without a hitch. I am not troubled by my first bath, nor by any later bath. Indeed, indeed you must believe me, it is not that at all.тАЭ

тАЬThe more they protest,тАЭ the psychoanalyst would murmur, тАЬthe more it is so.тАЭ For that was what Dr.┬аFreud and Dr.┬аJung always said, so that there was no escape from their aspersions.

тАЬWhy do you think you are so often unhappy?тАЭ he would ask her, to draw her out and she would reply, тАЬBecause my life is over. Because I am an old discarded woman, thrown away onto the dust-heap like a broken eggshell. Because my husband is gone and my children are gone, and they do not love me as I love them. Because I have only my mother to live with, and she is calm and cares for nothing but only waits for the end. Because I have nothing to do from morning till night. Because I am sixty-three, and that is too old and too young. Because life is empty and disappointing, and I am tired, and drift like seaweed tossed to and fro by the waves.тАЭ

It sounded indeed enough, and tears would fill her eyes as she said it. The psychoanalyst would listen, passive and sceptical but intelligent.

тАЬNot one of your reasons is the correct one. But I will find the true reason for you and expose it, and after that it will trouble you no more. Now you shall relate to me the whole history of your life.тАЭ

What a comfortable moment! Mrs.┬аHilary, when she came to it in her imagined interview, would draw a deep breath and settle down and begin. The story of her life! How absorbing a thing to relate to someone who really wanted to hear it! How far better than the confessionalтБатАФfor priests, besides requiring only those portions and parcels of the dreadful past upon which you had least desire to dwell, had almost certainly no interest at all in hearing even these, but only did it because they had to, and you would be boring them. They might even say, as one had said to Rosalind during the first confession which had inaugurated her brief ecclesiastical career, and to which she had looked forward with some interest as a luxurious reliving of a stimulating pastтБатАФтАЬNo details, please.тАЭ Rosalind, who had had many details ready, had come away disappointed, feeling that the Church was not all she had hoped. But the psychoanalyst doctor would really want to hear details. Of course he would prefer the kind of detail which Rosalind would have been able to furnish out of her experience, for that was what psychoanalysts recognised as true life. Mrs.┬аHilaryтАЩs experiences were pale in comparison; but psychoanalysts could and did make much out of little, bricks without clay. She would tell him all about the childrenтБатАФhow sweet they were as babies, how Jim had nearly died of croup, Neville of bronchitis and Nan of convulsions, whereas Pamela had always been so well, and Gilbert had suffered only from infant debility. She would relate how early and how unusually they had all given signs of intelligence; how Jim had always loved her more than anything in the world, until his marriage, and she him (this was a firm article in Mrs.┬аHilaryтАЩs creed); how Neville had always cherished and cared for her, and how she loved Neville beyond anything in the world but Jim; how Gilbert had disappointed her by taking to writing instead of to a manтАЩs job, and then by marrying Rosalind; how Nan had always been tiresome and perverse. And before the children cameтБатАФall about Richard, and their courtship, and their young married life, and how he had loved and cared for her beyond anything, incredibly tenderly and well, so that all those who saw it had wondered, and some had said he spoilt her. And back before Richard, to girlhood and childhood, to parents and nursery, to her brother and sister, now dead. How she had fought with her sister because they had both always wanted the same things and got in one anotherтАЩs way! The jealousies, the bitter, angry tears!

To pour it all outтБатАФwhat comfort! To feel that someone was interested, even though it might be only as a case. The trouble about most people was that they werenтАЩt interested. They didnтАЩt mostly, even pretend they were.

II

She tried Barry Briscoe, the weekend he came down and found Nan gone. Barry Briscoe was by way of being interested in people and things in general; he had that kind of alert mind and face.

He came up from the tennis lawn, where he had been playing a single with Rodney, and sat down by her and Grandmama in the shade of the cedar, hot and friendly and laughing and out of breath. Now Neville and Rodney were playing Gerda and Kay. GrandmamaтАЩs old eyes, pleased behind their glasses, watched the balls fly and thought everyone clever who got one over the net. She hadnтАЩt played tennis in her youth. Mrs.┬аHilaryтАЩs more eager, excited eyes watched Neville driving, smashing, volleying, returning, and thought how slim and young a thing she looked, to have all that power stored in her. She was fleeter than Gerda, she struck harder than Kay, she was trickier than all of them, the beloved girl. That was the way Mrs.┬аHilary watched tennis, thinking of the players, not of the play. It is the way some people talk, thinking of the talkers, not of what they are saying. It is the personal touch, and a way some women have.

But Barry Briscoe, watching cleverly through his bright glasses, was thinking of the strokes. He was an unconscious person. He lived in moments.

тАЬWell done, Gerda,тАЭ Grandmama would call, when Gerda, cool and nonchalant, dropped, a sitter at RodneyтАЩs feet, and when Rodney smashed it back she said, тАЬBut fatherтАЩs too much for you.тАЭ

тАЬGerdaтАЩs a scandal,тАЭ Barry said. тАЬShe doesnтАЩt care. She can hit all right when she likes. She thinks about something else half the time.тАЭ

His smile followed the small white figure with its bare golden head that gleamed in the grey afternoon. An absurd, lovable, teasable child, he found her.

GrandmamaтАЩs maid came to wheel her down to the farm. Grandmama had promised to go and see the farmerтАЩs wife and new baby. Grandmama always saw wives and new babies. They never palled. You would think that by eighty-four she had seen enough new babies, more than enough, that she had seen through that strange business and could now take it for granted, the stream of funny new life cascading into the already so full world. But Grandmama would always go and see it, handle it, admire it, peer at it with her smiling eyes that had seen so many lives come and go and that must know by now that babies are born to trouble as naturally as the sparks fly upward.

So off Grandmama rode in her wheeled chair, and Mrs.┬аHilary and Barry Briscoe were left alone. Mrs.┬аHilary and this pleasant, brown, friendly young man, who cared for WorkersтАЩ Education and Continuation Schools, and Penal Reform, and Garden Cities, and Getting Things Done by Acts of Parliament, about all which things Mrs.┬аHilary knew and cared nothing. But vaguely she felt that they sprang out of and must include a care for human beings as such, and that therefore Barry Briscoe would listen if she told him things.

So (it came out of lying on grass, which Barry was doing) she told him about the pneumonia of Neville as a child, how they had been staying in Cornwall, miles from a doctor, and without Mr.┬аHilary, and Mrs.┬аHilary had been in despair; how Jim, a little chap of twelve, had ridden off on his pony in the night to fetch the doctor, across the moors. A long story; stories about illnesses always are. Mrs.┬аHilary got worked up and excited as she told it; it came back to her so vividly, the dreadful night.

тАЬHe was a Dr.┬аChalmers, and so kind. When he saw Neville he was horrified; by that time she was delirious. He said if Jim hadnтАЩt gone straight to him but had waited till the morning, it might have been too late.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬToo late: quite.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ Barry Briscoe had an understanding, sympathetic grip of oneтАЩs last few words. So much of the conversation of others eludes one, but one should hold fast the last few words.

тАЬOh played, Gerda: did you that time, Bendish.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

Gerda had put on, probably by accident, a sudden, absurd twist that had made a fool of Rodney.

That was what Barry Briscoe was really attending to, the silly game. This alert, seemingly interested, attentive young man had a nice manner, that led you on, but he didnтАЩt really care. He lived in the moment: he cared for prisoners and workers, and probably for people who were ill now, but not that someone had been ill all those years ago. He only pretended to care; he was polite. He turned his keen, pleasant face up to her when he had done shouting about the game, and said тАЬHow splendid that he got to you in time!тАЭ but he didnтАЩt really care. Mrs.┬аHilary found that women were better listeners than men. Women are perhaps better trained; they think it more ill-mannered not to show interest. They will listen to stories about servants, or reports of the inane sayings of infants, they will hear you through, without the flicker of a yawn, but with ejaculations and noddings, while you tell them about your childrenтАЩs diseases. They are well-bred; they drive themselves on a tight rein, and endure. They are the worldтАЩs martyrs.

But men, less restrained, will fidget and wander and sigh and yawn, and change the subject.

To trap and hold the sympathy of a manтБатАФhow wonderful! Who wanted a pack of women? What you really wanted was some man whose trade it was to listen and to give heed. Some man to whom your daughterтАЩs pneumonia, of however long ago, was not irrelevant, but had its own significance, as having helped to build you up as you were, you, the problem, with your wonderful, puzzling temperament, so full of complexes, inconsistencies and needs. Some man who didnтАЩt lose interest in you just because you were grey-haired and sixty-three.

тАЬIтАЩm afraid IтАЩve been taking your attention from the game,тАЭ said Mrs.┬аHilary to Barry Briscoe.

Compunction stabbed him. Had he been rude to this elderly lady, who had been telling him a long tale without a point while he watched the tennis and made polite, attentive sounds?

тАЬNot a bit, Mrs.┬аHilary.тАЭ He sat up, and looked friendlier than ever. тАЬIтАЩve been thrilled.тАЭ A charming, easy liar Barry was, when he deemed it necessary. His Quaker parents would have been shocked. But there was truth in it, after all. For people were so interested in themselves, that one was, in a sense, interested in the stories they told one, even stories about illness. Besides, this was the mother of Nan; Nan, who was so abruptly and inexplicably not here today, whose absence was hurting him, when he stopped to think, like an aching tooth; for he was not sure, yet feared, what she meant by it.

тАЬTell me,тАЭ he said, half to please NanтАЩs mother and half on his own account, тАЬsome stories of Nan when she was small. I should think she was a fearful child.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

He was interested, thought Mrs.┬аHilary, in Nan, but not in her. That was natural, of course. No man would ever again want to hear stories of her childhood. The familiar bitterness rose and beat in her like a wave. Nan was thirty-four and she was sixty-three. She could talk only of far-off things, and theories about conduct and life which sounded all right at first but were exposed after two minutes as not having behind them the background of any knowledge or any brain. That hadnтАЩt mattered when she was a girl; men would often rather they hadnтАЩt. But at sixty-three you have nothing.тБатАКтБатАж The bitter emptiness of sixty-three turned her sick with frustration. Life was over, over, over, for her and she was to tell stories of Nan, who had everything.

Then the mother in her rose up, to claim and grasp for her child, even for the child she loved least.

тАЬNan? Nan was always a most dreadfully sensitive child, and temperamental. She took after me, IтАЩm afraid; the others were more like their father. I remember when she was quite a little thing.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

Barry had asked for it. But he hadnтАЩt known that, out of the brilliant, uncertain Nan, exciting as a Punch and Judy show, anything so tedious could be spun.тБатАКтБатАж

III

Mrs.┬аHilary was up in town by herself for a dayтАЩs shopping. The sales were on at BarkerтАЩs and Derry and TomтАЩs. Mrs.┬аHilary wandered about these shops, and even PontingтАЩs and bought little bags, and presents for everyone, remnants, oddments, underwear, some green silk for a frock for Gerda, a shady hat for herself, a wonderful cushion for Grandmama with a picture of the sea on it, a silk knitted jumper for Neville, of the same purplish blue as her eyes. She was happy, going about like a bee from flower to flower, gathering this honey for them all. She had come up alone; she hadnтАЩt let Neville come with her. She had said she was going to be an independent old woman. But what she really meant was that she had proposed herself for tea with Rosalind in Campden Hill Square, and wanted to be alone for that.

Rosalind had been surprised, for Mrs.┬аHilary seldom favoured her with a visit. She had found the letter on the hall table when she and Gilbert had come in from a dinner party two evenings ago.

тАЬYour motherтАЩs coming to tea on Thursday, Gilbert. Tea with me. She says she wants a talk. I feel flattered. She says nothing about wanting to see you, so youтАЩd better leave us alone, anyhow for a bit.тАЭ

RosalindтАЩs beautiful bistre-brown eyes smiled. She enjoyed her talks with her mother-in-law; they furnished her with excellent material, to be worked up later by the raconteuseтАЩs art into something too delicious and absurd. She enjoyed, too, telling Mrs.┬аHilary the latest scandals; she was so shocked and disgusted; and it was fun dropping little accidental hints about Nan, and even about Gilbert. Anyhow, what a treasure of a relic of the Victorian age! And how comic in her jealousy, her ingenuous, futile boasting, her so readily exposed deceits! And how she hated Rosalind herself, the painted, corrupt woman who was dragging Gilbert down!

тАЬWhatever does she want a talk about?тАЭ Rosalind wondered. тАЬIt must be something pretty urgent, to make her put up with an hour of my company.тАЭ

IV

At four oтАЩclock on Thursday afternoon Rosalind went upstairs and put on an extra coating of powder and rouge. She also blackened her eyelashes and put on her lips salve the colour of strawberries rather than of the human mouth. She wore an afternoon dress with transparent black sleeves through which her big arms gleamed, pale and smooth. She looked a superb and altogether improper creature, like Lucrezia Borgia or a Titian madonna. She came down and lay among great black and gold satin cushions, and lit a scented cigarette and opened a new French novel. Black and gold was her new scheme for her drawing-room; she had had it done this spring. It had a sort of opulent and rakish violence which suited her ripe magnificence, her splendid flesh tints, her brown eyes and corn-gold hair. Against it she looked like Messalina, and Gilbert like rather a decadent and cynical pope. The note of the room was really too pronounced for GilbertтАЩs fastidious and scholarly eloquence; he lost vitality in it, and dwindled to the pale thin casket of a brain.

And Mrs.┬аHilary, when she entered it, trailing in, tall and thin, in her sagging grey coat and skirt, her wispy grey hair escaping from under her floppy black hat, and with the air of having till a moment ago been hung about with parcels (she had left them in the hall), looked altogether unsuited to her environment, like a dowdy lady from the provinces, as she was.

Rosalind came forward and took her by the hands.

тАЬWell, mother dear, this is an unusual honour.тБатАКтБатАж How long is it since we last had you here?тАЭ

Rosalind, enveloping her mother-in-law in extravagant fragrance, kissed her on each cheek. The kiss of Messalina! Mrs.┬аHilary glanced at the great mirror over the fireplace to see whether it had come off on her cheeks, as it might well have done.

Rosalind placed her on a swelling, billowy, black and gold chair, piled cushions behind her shoulders, made her lie back at an obtuse angle, a grey, lank, elderly figure, strange in that opulent setting, her long dusty black feet stretched out before her on the golden carpet.

Desperately uncomfortable and angular Rosalind made you feel, petting you and purring over you and calling you тАЬmother dear,тАЭ with that glint always behind her golden-brown eyes which showed that she was up to no good, that she knew you hated her and was only leading you on that she might strike her claws into you the deeper. The great beautiful cat: that was what Rosalind was. You didnтАЩt trust her for a moment.

She was pouring out tea.

тАЬLemon? But how dreadfully stupid of me! IтАЩd forgotten you take milkтБатАКтБатАж oh yes, and sugar.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

She rang, and ordered sugar. Mothers take it; not the mothers of RosalindтАЩs world, but mothersтАЩ meetings, and school treats, and mothers-in-law up from the seaside.

тАЬAre you up for shopping? How thrilling! Where have you been?тБатАКтБатАж Oh, High Street. Did you find anything there?тАЭ

Mrs.┬аHilary knew that Rosalind would see her off, hung over with dozens of parcels, and despise them, knowing that if they were so many they must also be cheap.

тАЬOh, thereтАЩs not much to be got there, of course,тАЭ she said. тАЬI got a few little thingsтБатАФchiefly for my mother to give away in the parish. She likes to have things.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬBut how noble of you both! IтАЩm afraid I never rise to that. ItтАЩs all I can manage to give presents to myself and nearest rellies. And you came up to town just to get presents for the parish! YouтАЩre wonderful, mother!тАЭ

тАЬOh, I take a day in town now and then. Why not? Everyone does.тАЭ

Extraordinary how defiant Rosalind made one feel, prying and questioning and trying to make one look absurd.

тАЬWhy, of course! It freshens you up, I expect; makes a change.тБатАКтБатАж But youтАЩve come up from Windover, havenтАЩt you, not the seaside?тАЭ

Rosalind always called St.┬аMaryтАЩs Bay the seaside. To her our island coasts were all one; the seaside was where you went to bathe, and she hardly distinguished between north, south, east and west.

тАЬHow are they down at Windover? I heard that Nan was there, with that young man of hers who performs good works. So unlike Nan herself! I hope she isnтАЩt going to be so silly as to let it come to anything; theyтАЩd both be miserable. But I should think Nan knows better than to marry a square-toes. I daresay he knows better too, really.тБатАКтБатАж And howтАЩs poor old Neville? I think this doctoring game of hers is simply a scream, the poor old dear.тАЭ

To hear Rosalind discussing Neville.тБатАКтБатАж Messalina coarsely patronising a wood-nymphтБатАКтБатАж the cat striking her claws into a singing bird.тБатАКтБатАж And poorтБатАФand old! Neville was, indeed, six years ahead of Rosalind, but she looked the younger of the two, in her slim activity, and didnтАЩt need to paint her face either. Mrs.┬аHilary all but said so.

тАЬIt is a great interest to Neville, taking up her medical studies again,тАЭ was all she could really say. (What a hampering thing it is to be a lady!) тАЬShe thoroughly enjoys it, and looks younger than ever. She is playing a lot of tennis, and beats them all.тАЭ

How absurdly her voice rang when she spoke of Neville or Jim! It always made RosalindтАЩs lip curl mockingly.

тАЬWonderful creature! I do admire her. When IтАЩm her age I shall be too fat to take any exercise at all. I think itтАЩs splendid of women who keep it up through the forties.тБатАКтБатАж She wonтАЩt be bored, even when sheтАЩs sixty, will she?тАЭ

That was a direct hit, which Mrs.┬аHilary could bear better than hits at Neville.

тАЬI see no reason,тАЭ said Mrs.┬аHilary, тАЬwhy Neville should ever be bored. She has a husband and children. Long before she is sixty she will have KayтАЩs and GerdaтАЩs children to be interested in.тАЭ

тАЬNo, I suppose one canтАЩt well be bored if one has grandchildren, can one,тАЭ Rosalind said, reflectively.

There was a silence, during which Mrs.┬аHilaryтАЩs eyes, coldly meeting RosalindтАЩs with their satirical comment, said тАЬI know you are too selfish a woman ever to bear children, and I thank God for it. Little Hilarys who should be half yours would be more than I could endure.тАЭ

Rosalind, quite understanding, smiled her slow, full-mouthed, curling smile, and held out to her mother-in-law the gold case with scented cigarettes.

тАЬOh no, you donтАЩt, do you. I never can remember that. ItтАЩs so unusual.тАЭ

Her eyes travelled over Mrs.┬аHilary, from her dusty black shoes to her pale, lined face. They put her, with deliberation, into the class with companions, housekeepers, poor relations. Having successfully done that (she knew it was successful, by Mrs.┬аHilaryтАЩs faint flush) she said тАЬYou donтАЩt look up to much, mother dear. Not as if Neville had been looking after you very well.тАЭ

Mrs.┬аHilary, seeing her chance, swallowed her natural feelings and took it.

тАЬThe fact is, I sleep very badly. Not particularly just now, but always.тБатАКтБатАж I thought.тБатАКтБатАж That is, someone told meтБатАКтБатАж that there have been wonderful cures for insomnia latelyтБатАКтБатАж through that new thing.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

тАЬWhich new thing? Sedobrol? Paraldehyd? Gilbert keeps getting absurd powders and tablets of all sorts. Thank God, I always sleep like a top.тАЭ

тАЬNo, not those. The thing you practice. Psychoanalysis, I mean.тАЭ

тАЬOh, psycho. But you wouldnтАЩt touch that, surely? I thought it was anathema.тАЭ

тАЬBut if it really does cure people.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ

RosalindтАЩs eyes glittered and gleamed. Her strawberry-red mouth curled joyfully.

тАЬOf course it has.тБатАКтБатАж Not that insomnia is always a case for psycho, you know. ItтАЩs sometimes incipient mania.тАЭ

тАЬNot in my case.тАЭ Mrs.┬аHilary spoke sharply.

тАЬWhy no, of course not.тБатАКтБатАж Well, I think youтАЩd be awfully wise to get analysed. Whom do you want to go to?тАЭ

тАЬI thought you could tell me. I know no names.тБатАКтБатАж A man,тАЭ Mrs.┬аHilary added quickly.

тАЬOh, it must be a man? I was going to say, IтАЩve a vacancy myself for a patient. But women usually want men doctors. They nearly all do. ItтАЩs supposed to be part of the complaint.тБатАКтБатАж Well, I could fix you up a preliminary interview with Dr.┬аClaude Evans. HeтАЩs very good. He turns you right inside out and shows you everything about yourself, from your first infant passion to the thoughts you think youтАЩre keeping dark from him as you sit in the consulting room. HeтАЩs great.тАЭ

Mrs.┬аHilary was flushed. Hope and shame tingled in her together.

тАЬI shanтАЩt want to keep anything dark. IтАЩve no reason.тАЭ

RosalindтАЩs mocking eyes said тАЬThatтАЩs what they all say.тАЭ Her lips said тАЬThe foreconscious self always has its reasons for hiding up the things the unconscious self knows and feels.тАЭ

тАЬOh, all that stuff.тБатАКтБатАжтАЭ Mrs.┬аHilary was sick of it, having read too much about it in The Breath of Life. тАЬI hope this Dr.┬аEvans will talk to me in plain English, not in that affected jargon.тАЭ

тАЬHeтАЩll use language suited to you, I suppose,тАЭ said Rosalind, тАЬas far as he can. But these things canтАЩt always be put so that just anyone can grasp them. TheyтАЩre too complicated. You should read it up beforehand, and try if you can understand it a little.тАЭ

Rosalind, who had no brains herself, insulting Mrs.┬аHilaryтАЩs, was rather more than Mrs.┬аHilary could bear. Rosalind she knew for a fool, so far as intellectual matters went, for Nan had said so. Clever enough at clothes, and talking scandal, and winning money at games, and skating over thin ice without going throughтБатАФbut when it came to a book, or an idea, or a political question, Rosalind was no whit more intelligent than she was, in fact much less. She was a rotten psychoanalyst, all her in-laws were sure.

Mrs.┬аHilary said, тАЬIтАЩve been reading a good deal about it lately. It doesnтАЩt seem to me very difficult, though exceedingly foolish in parts.тАЭ

Rosalind was touchy about psychoanalysis; she always got angry if people said it was foolish in any way. She was like that; she could see no weak points in anything she took up; it came from being vain, and not having a brain. She said one of the things angry people say, instead of discussing the subject rationally.

тАЬI donтАЩt suppose the amount of it youтАЩve been able to read would seem difficult. If you came to anything difficult youтАЩd probably stop, you see. Anyhow, if it seems to you so foolish why do you want to be analysed?тАЭ

тАЬOh, one may as well try things. IтАЩve no doubt thereтАЩs something in it besides the nonsense.тАЭ

Mrs.┬аHilary spoke jauntily, with hungry, unquiet, seeking eyes that would not meet RosalindтАЩs. She was afraid that Rosalind would find out that she wanted to be cured of being miserable, of being jealous, of having inordinate passions about so little. Rosalind, in some ways a great stupid cow, was uncannily clever when it came to being spiteful and knowing about you the things you didnтАЩt want known. It must be horrible to be psychoanalysed by Rosalind, who had no pity and no reticence. The things about you would not only be known but spread abroad among all those whom Rosalind met. A vile, dreadful tongue.

тАЬYou wouldnтАЩt, I expect, like me to analyse you,тАЭ said Rosalind. тАЬNot a course, I mean, but just once, to advise you better whom to go to. ItтАЩd have the advantage, anyhow, that IтАЩd do it free. Anyone else will charge you three guineas at the least.тАЭ

тАЬI donтАЩt think,тАЭ said Mrs.┬аHilary, тАЬthat relationsтБатАФor connectionsтБатАФought to do one another. No, IтАЩd better go to someone I donтАЩt know, if youтАЩll give me the name and address.тАЭ

тАЬI thought youтАЩd probably rather,тАЭ Rosalind said in her slow, soft, cruel voice, like a catтАЩs purr. тАЬWell, IтАЩll write down the address for you. ItтАЩs Dr.┬аEvans: heтАЩll probably pass you on to someone down at the seaside, if he considers you a suitable case for treatment.тАЭ

He would; of course he would. Mrs.┬аHilary felt no doubt as to that.

Gilbert came in from the British Museum. He looked thin and nervous and sallow amid all the splendour. He kissed his mother, thinking how queer and untidy she looked, a stranger and pilgrim in RosalindтАЩs drawing-room. He too might look there at times a stranger and pilgrim, but at least, if not voluptuous, he was neat. He glanced proudly and yet ironically from his mother to his magnificent wife, taking in and understanding the supra-normal redundancies of her makeup.

тАЬRosalind,тАЭ said Mrs.┬аHilary, knowing that it would be less than useless to ask Rosalind to keep her secret, тАЬhas been recommending me a psychoanalyst doctor. I think it is worth while trying if I can get my insomnia cured that way.тАЭ

тАЬMy dear mother! After all your fulminations against the tribe! Well, I think youтАЩre quite right to give it a trial. Why donтАЩt you get Rosalind to take you on?тАЭ

The fond pride in his voice! Yet there was in his eyes, as they rested for a moment on Rosalind, something other than fond pride; something more like mockery.

Mrs.┬аHilary got up to go, and fired across the rich room the one shot in her armoury.

тАЬI believe,тАЭ she said, тАЬthat Rosalind prefers chiefly to take men patients. She wouldnтАЩt want to be bored with an old woman.тАЭ

The shot drove straight into GilbertтАЩs light-strung sensitiveness. Shell-shocked officers; any other officers; anything male, presentable and passably young; these were RosalindтАЩs patients; he knew it, and everyone else knew it. For a moment his smile was fixed into the deliberate grin of pain. Mrs.┬аHilary saw it, saw Gilbert far back down the years, a small boy standing up to punishment with just that brave, nervous grin. Sensitive, defiant, vulnerable, fastidiously proudтБатАФso Gilbert had always been and always would be.

Remorsefully she clung to him.

тАЬCome and see me out, dearest boyтАЭ (so she called him, though Jim was really that)тБатАФand she ignored RosalindтАЩs slow, unconcerned protest against her last remark. тАЬWhy, mother, you know I asked to do youтАЭтБатАКтБатАж but she couldnтАЩt prevent Rosalind from seeing her out too, hanging her about with all the ridiculous parcels, kissing her on both cheeks.

Gilbert was cool and dry, pretending she hadnтАЩt hurt him. He would always take hurts like that, with that deadly, steely lightness. By its deadliness, its steeliness, she knew that it was all true (and much more besides) that she had heard about Rosalind and her patients.

V

She walked down to the bus with hot eyes. Rosalind had yawned softly and largely behind her as she went down the front steps. Wicked, monstrous creature! Lying about GilbertтАЩs clever, nervous, eager life in great soft folds, and throttling it. If Gilbert had been a man, a real male man, instead of a writer and therefore effeminate, decadent, he would have beaten her into decent behaviour. As it was she would ruin him, and he would go under, not able to bear it, but cynically grinning still. Perhaps the sooner the better. Anything was better than the way Rosalind went on now, disgracing him and getting talked about, and making him hate his mother for disliking her. He hadnтАЩt even come with her to the bus, to carry her parcels for her.тБатАКтБатАж That wasnтАЩt like Gilbert. As a rule he had excellent manners, though he was not affectionate like Jim.

Jim, Jim, Jim. Should she go to Harley Street? What was the use? She would find only Margery there; Jim would be out. Margery had no serious faults except the one, that she had taken the first place in JimтАЩs affections. Before Margery, Neville had had this place, but Mrs.┬аHilary had been able, with NevilleтАЩs never failing and skilful help, to disguise this from herself. You canтАЩt disguise a wifeтАЩs place in her husbandтАЩs heart. And JimтАЩs splendid children too, whom she adoredтБатАФthey looked at her with MargeryтАЩs brown eyes instead of JimтАЩs grey-blue ones. And they preferred really (she knew it) their maternal grandmother, the jolly lady who took them to the theatres.

Mrs.┬аHilary passed a church. Religion. Some people found help there. But it required so much of you, was so exhausting in its demands. Besides, it seemed infinitely far awayтБатАФan improbable, sad, remote thing, that gave you no human comfort. Psychoanalysis was better; that opened gates into a new life. тАЬKnow thyself,тАЭ Mrs.┬аHilary murmured, kindling at the prospect. Most knowledge was dull, but never that.

тАЬI will ring up from Waterloo and make an appointment,тАЭ she thought.
